jsonLdWeb.name: Brick Calculator
jsonLdWeb.description: Calculate bricks, mortar bags, and wall ties for any wall project.

mainEntity.name: How many bricks per square foot?

acceptedAnswer.text: With standard modular bricks (3.625" × 2.25" × 8") and 3/8" mortar joints, you need approximately 6.75 bricks per square foot. With 1/2" mortar joints, it is about 6.16 bricks per square foot. These numbers are for a single wythe (one brick thick) wall in running bond pattern.

mainEntity.name: How do I calculate bricks for a wall?

acceptedAnswer.text: Multiply wall length by height to get area in square feet. Subtract any openings (doors, windows). Multiply net area by bricks per square foot (6.75 for standard bricks with 3/8" joints). Add 5-10% for waste from cutting and breakage. For example: 20 ft × 8 ft wall = 160 sq ft × 6.75 = 1,080 bricks + 5% waste = 1,134 bricks.

mainEntity.name: How much mortar do I need?

acceptedAnswer.text: One 80 lb bag of mortar mix lays approximately 35-40 standard bricks with 3/8" joints. For larger 1/2" joints, a bag covers about 30 bricks. For a wall of 1,000 bricks, plan on about 27-29 bags of mortar mix. Type S mortar is standard for structural walls; Type N for non-load-bearing.

mainEntity.name: What size is a standard brick?

acceptedAnswer.text: The standard modular brick in the US is 3.625" wide × 2.25" tall × 8" long (actual dimensions). With 3/8" mortar joints, the nominal (module) dimensions are 4" × 2.667" × 8.375", which courses to 3 bricks per 8 inches of height and works with 4-inch module construction.

mainEntity.name: What are wall ties and how many do I need?

acceptedAnswer.text: Wall ties (brick ties) connect a brick veneer to the structural wall behind it. They are typically corrugated metal strips or wire ties. Install one tie per 2.67 square feet of wall area — approximately every 16" horizontally and every 16" vertically (every 6 courses). Additional ties are needed within 12 inches of all openings.

mainEntity.name: How much waste should I plan for?

acceptedAnswer.text: Plan for 5% waste on straight walls with few cuts. Add 10% for walls with many openings or complex patterns. Arches and angled walls may need 15%+. Bricks from different production lots may vary in color, so order all bricks from the same lot and add waste to avoid reordering.

howItWorks:
How to Calculate Bricks

Brick calculations are based on the wall area divided by the area each brick covers (including its mortar joint). The result is multiplied by a waste factor for cuts and breakage.

Bricks = Net Area × Bricks/SqFt × (1 + Waste%)
  • Gross area = wall length × wall height
  • Net area = gross area − openings (doors, windows)
  • Bricks per sq ft = based on brick size + mortar joint (~6.75 for standard with 3/8" joint)
  • Mortar: 1 bag (80 lb) lays ~35–40 bricks
  • Wall ties: 1 per ~2.67 sq ft (16"×16" grid)
  • Add 5–10% waste for cuts and breakage
faqHTML:
Frequently Asked Questions

How many bricks per square foot?

With standard modular bricks and 3/8" mortar joints: about 6.75 per sq ft. With 1/2" joints: about 6.16 per sq ft. This is for a single-wythe wall in running bond pattern.

How do I calculate bricks for a wall?

Wall length × height = gross area. Subtract openings. Multiply net area by bricks/sq ft. Add 5-10% waste. Example: 20×8 ft wall minus 36 sq ft openings = 124 sq ft × 6.75 = 837 + 5% = 879 bricks.

How much mortar do I need?

One 80 lb bag lays ~35-40 bricks with 3/8" joints. For 1,000 bricks, plan on 27-29 bags. Type S mortar for structural walls; Type N for non-bearing.

What size is a standard brick?

US standard modular: 3.625" × 2.25" × 8" (actual). With 3/8" mortar, nominal dimensions are 4" × 2.667" × 8.375", coursing to 3 bricks per 8" of height.

How many wall ties do I need?

One tie per 2.67 sq ft — every 16" horizontally and 16" vertically (every 6 courses). Extra ties within 12" of all openings.

How much waste to plan for?

5% for straight walls. 10% for many openings. 15% for arches and complex patterns. Order from one lot to ensure color consistency.

howWeCalculate.intro: The calculator works in square feet and converts to bricks using the coverage rate for the brick size and mortar joint you pick. For a standard modular brick with a 3/8" joint, that rate is about 6.75 bricks per square foot for a single-wythe wall.
steps: Multiply wall length by height to get the gross area, then subtract the square footage of doors and windows to get the net area — a 20 ft × 8 ft wall with a 36 sq ft opening nets 124 sq ft.
Multiply the net area by the bricks-per-square-foot rate for your chosen brick and joint (6.75 for standard modular with a 3/8" joint), which turns 124 sq ft into about 837 bricks.
Apply the waste allowance — at 5% those 837 bricks round up to about 879 to cover cuts and breakage.
Divide the brick count by 35–40 to size the 80 lb mortar bags, and divide the net area by 2.67 to size wall ties for a veneer.
howWeCalculate.disclaimer: These figures assume a single-wythe running-bond wall and a full brick every course. A double-wythe (two-brick-thick) wall doubles the brick and mortar; piers, arches, and soldier courses use more. Confirm quantities against your brick supplier's coverage chart before ordering.

whenToUse.intro: Reach for this when you know a wall's footprint and need to turn it into a brick order, a mortar count, and a rough materials cost.
scenarios: Pricing a brick veneer on a house so you can hand the supplier a count instead of a guess.
Building a freestanding garden or retaining wall and want to know how many pallets to have delivered.
Sizing a single-wythe planter or low border wall where a few square feet of openings still change the total.
Checking a mason's material line on a quote — bricks, mortar bags, and ties — before you sign it.
Reordering after a first course goes down and you realize the wall has more cuts than you planned for.

sections.title: From Wall Size to Brick Count
sections.body: A brick order starts with area, not brick counts. Measure the wall in feet, multiply length by height, and you have square feet to work with. A single-wythe wall of standard modular bricks with 3/8" mortar joints takes about 7 bricks for every square foot once you account for the joint around each unit. A 10 ft × 8 ft wall is 80 sq ft; at ~7 bricks per sq ft that's 560 bricks, or about 616 once you add 10% for waste. Enter your dimensions above and the calculator does the same arithmetic for whatever brick size and joint you choose.

sections.title: Openings, Waste, and Double-Wythe Walls
sections.body: Two things change the raw count. First, subtract openings: a 3 ft × 6 ft door and a couple of windows can easily be 36 sq ft, which is roughly 250 bricks you don't buy. Second, add waste — 5% for a long straight wall, 10% when there are many cut edges around openings, and 15% for arches or herringbone patterns where partial bricks pile up. If the wall is two bricks thick (double-wythe), double both the brick and the mortar; a structural cavity wall is effectively two single-wythe walls tied together.

sections.title: Mortar and Wall Ties Come With the Bricks
sections.body: Bricks are only part of the order. One 80 lb bag of mortar mix lays roughly 35–40 standard bricks with 3/8" joints, so a 1,000-brick wall needs about 27–29 bags — buy Type S for load-bearing work and Type N for a non-structural veneer. If you're facing a wood-framed wall with a brick veneer, you also need wall ties at about one per 2.67 sq ft (a 16" × 16" grid), plus extra ties within a foot of every opening. The calculator reports all three so the quantities on your delivery match the wall you're actually building.

howToSteps.title: Measure the wall in feet
howToSteps.description: Enter wall length and height. Length × height gives the gross area — a 20 ft long, 8 ft tall wall is 160 sq ft before openings.

howToSteps.title: Pick the brick size and joint
howToSteps.description: Choose standard modular, queen, king, or utility, and set the mortar joint to 3/8" or 1/2". A bigger brick or thicker joint means fewer bricks per square foot.

howToSteps.title: Subtract openings and set waste
howToSteps.description: Enter the total square footage of doors and windows to deduct, then choose a waste allowance — 5% for straight walls, 10% around many openings, 15% for complex patterns.

howToSteps.title: Read the brick, mortar, and tie counts
howToSteps.description: The results show bricks needed with waste included, 80 lb mortar bags, wall ties, and net wall area — plus an optional cost if you enter a price per brick.

tips: Measure each opening and add them up before entering the deduction — a door and two windows are often 30–40 sq ft, which is a few hundred bricks.
For a double-wythe or cavity wall, run the calculator once and double the brick and mortar totals; the tie count grows too.
Order all your bricks in one delivery from the same production lot — colors drift between lots, and a mid-wall reorder rarely matches.
Bump waste to 10–15% if the wall has arches, angled ends, or a soldier course, since those chew through partial bricks.
Round mortar bags up, not down — running short mid-course means the joints you already laid start to set before you can tool them.

faq.question: How many bricks do I need per square foot?
faq.answer: For a single-wythe wall of standard modular bricks with 3/8" mortar joints, plan on about 6.75–7 bricks per square foot. With 1/2" joints it drops to roughly 6.16. Larger bricks cover more: a queen is about 4.6 per sq ft and a utility about 3.

faq.question: How much waste should I add to a brick order?
faq.answer: 5% is enough for a long straight wall with few cuts. Use 10% when there are many openings, since every window and door adds cut edges, and 15% for arches, herringbone, or angled walls where partial bricks accumulate. It's cheaper to have a few spares than to reorder from a different lot.

faq.question: Do I subtract doors and windows?
faq.answer: Yes. Add up the square footage of every opening and deduct it from the gross wall area before multiplying by bricks per square foot. A 3 ft × 6 ft door is 18 sq ft — about 125 standard bricks you don't need to buy. The calculator has a field for total openings.

faq.question: How much mortar do I need for the wall?
faq.answer: One 80 lb bag of mortar mix lays roughly 35–40 standard bricks with 3/8" joints, or about 30 with 1/2" joints. A 1,000-brick wall needs about 27–29 bags. Use Type S mortar for load-bearing walls and Type N for non-structural veneer.

faq.question: What's the difference between a single-wythe and double-wythe wall?
faq.answer: A single wythe is one brick thick — the default this calculator uses. A double-wythe (two-brick-thick) wall, common for freestanding garden and retaining walls, needs twice the bricks and twice the mortar. Run the numbers once and double them, and add ties if the two wythes are joined across a cavity.

faq.question: How many wall ties does a brick veneer need?
faq.answer: About one tie per 2.67 square feet of veneer — roughly a 16" × 16" grid, or every sixth course horizontally spaced 16" apart. Add extra ties within 12 inches of every window, door, and corner. A 124 sq ft veneer works out to around 47 ties before the extras at openings.
